records.ety: [AS. rīdan; akin to LG. riden, D. rijden, G. reiten, OHG. rītan, Icel. rīða, Sw. rida, Dan. ride; cf. L. raeda a carriage, which is from a Celtic word. Cf. Road.]
records.morph: [imp. Rode (rōd) (Rid [rd], archaic); p. p. Ridden (<?/) (Rid, archaic); p. pr. & vb. n. Riding (<?/).]
usage: -- Ride, Drive. Ride originally meant (and is so used throughout the English Bible) to be carried on horseback or in a vehicle of any kind. At present in England, drive is the word applied in most cases to progress in a carriage; as, a drive around the park, etc.; while ride is appropriated to progress on a horse. Johnson seems to sanction this distinction by giving “to travel on horseback” as the leading sense of ride; though he adds “to travel in a vehicle” as a secondary sense. This latter use of the word still occurs to some extent; as, the queen rides to Parliament in her coach of state; to ride in an omnibus.

defs: To be carried on the back of an animal, as a horse.
quotes: To-morrow, when ye riden by the way.
Let your master ride on before, and do you gallop after him.

defs: To be borne in a carriage; as, to ride in a coach, in a car, and the like. See Synonym, below.
quotes: The richest inhabitants exhibited their wealth, not by riding in gilden carriages, but by walking the streets with trains of servants.

defs: To be borne or in a fluid; to float; to lie.
quotes: Men once walked where ships at anchor ride.

defs: To be supported in motion; to rest.
quotes: Strong as the exletree On which heaven rides.
On whose foolish honesty My practices ride easy!

defs: To manage a horse, as an equestrian.
quotes: He rode, he fenced, he moved with graceful ease.

defs: To support a rider, as a horse; to move under the saddle; as, a horse rides easy or hard, slow or fast.
quotes: “Will you ride over or drive?” said Lord Willowby to his quest, after breakfast that morning.

defs: To sit on, so as to be carried; as, to ride a horse; to ride a bicycle.
quotes: [They] rend up both rocks and hills, and ride the air In whirlwind.

defs: To manage insolently at will; to domineer over.
quotes: The nobility could no longer endure to be ridden by bakers, cobblers, and brewers.

defs: To convey, as by riding; to make or do by riding.
quotes: Tue only men that safe can ride Mine errands on the Scottish side.

defs: To overlap (each other); -- said of bones or fractured fragments.
The act of riding; an excursion on horseback or in a vehicle.
A road or avenue cut in a wood, or through grounds, to be used as a place for riding; a riding.
